nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; generally found over open muddy, sandy, or silty substrates in protected bays and estuaries, around rock outcrops, wreckage, and rubble, as well as on shallow coastal coral reefs at depths of roughly 1-50 m (recorded as deep as ~170 m). Often encountered in turbid water, singly or in small groups; juveniles tend to shelter in weedy zones.

decorPreferences: live rock arranged into caves and ledges for daytime shelter

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding on benthic invertebrates (crustaceans, mollusks, worms) and small fishes, foraging primarily at night. In captivity newly imported specimens are commonly slow to feed and may need to be enticed with live saltwater shrimp (or freshwater ghost shrimp as a temporary substitute); once feeding, generally accepts a varied carnivore diet of chopped shrimp, squid, clam, mussel, krill, mysis, marine fish flesh, and prepared frozen carnivore foods. Multiple small meaty feedings per day are typically recommended given the species' eventual large size and rapid growth.

avoidWith: very small fish that could be swallowed
highly aggressive tankmates that stress juveniles

summary: The Painted Sweetlips (Diagramma pictum) is a peaceful Indo-Pacific grunt that has been reported to grow up to roughly 100 cm. Juveniles show bold black-and-white stripes that transform into a blue-grey body with yellow spotting in adults. Suited only to very large fish-only systems.
careNotes:
The Painted Sweetlips, also encountered under the older combinations Plectorhinchus pictus and Spilotichthys pictus, is a large Indo-Pacific haemulid that is generally considered peaceful but is recommended only for expert keepers due to its eventual size and rapid growth. Adults have been reported to reach about 100 cm total length (common length around 55 cm), making it impractical for all but the largest private aquariums and public displays.

The species shows a striking juvenile-to-adult color change. Juveniles display bold alternating black-and-white stripes with silvery-yellow underparts and yellow fins marked with black blotches; adults shift to a predominantly blue-grey body covered in bright yellow to golden-orange spots that form line patterns over the head, with a vivid orange-red mouth interior. Buyers should be aware that the small striped juvenile they purchase will look very different at adult size.

Feeding is the most commonly cited husbandry challenge. Like other sweetlips, newly imported specimens are often slow or reluctant to feed and have been known to require live shrimp to initiate feeding; once eating, individuals generally transition to a varied frozen and prepared meaty diet. Multiple small feedings per day are commonly advised given the species' rapid growth.

Reef compatibility is best treated 'with caution': the fish is not aggressive toward tankmates of comparable size but will hunt smaller crustaceans, worms, and small fishes consistent with its wild diet, making it generally unsuitable for typical reef setups. Fish-only-with-live-rock systems featuring open swimming space and caves/ledges for daytime shelter are preferred. The species is largely nocturnal in the wild, foraging over open substrate at night, and like other Haemulidae produces audible grunting sounds via pharyngeal teeth.

Source notes: LiveAquaria lists the species under the older name Plectorhynchus pictus with a 50-gallon minimum tank, Easy care level, Peaceful temperament, Reef Compatible Yes, and parameters 72-78°F (22.2-25.6°C), pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025; however LiveAquaria's own overview prose notes the fish 'can grow quite large, up to 33 inches' and is a nocturnal bottom-feeder that threatens small fish and invertebrates, contradicting the listed tank size and reef rating. FishBase reports max size 100 cm FL (common 55 cm), depth 1-50 m, preferred temperature 24.1-29.1°C (mean 28°C), maximum reported age 13 years, and a diet of benthic invertebrates and fishes. Wikipedia corroborates the 100 cm maximum (55 cm typical, 6.3 kg), the wide Indo-Pacific range from the Red Sea to Fiji, and the juvenile-to-adult color transformation. Given the documented adult size, the minimum tank volume here has been raised well above the LiveAquaria figure to 2000 L following the playbook's larger-tank bias for large active swimmers; care level has been set to expert in line with adult size and feeding-initiation difficulty rather than LiveAquaria's 'Easy' rating, which appears to reflect juvenile-only husbandry.
breedingNotes: Wild spawning is reported as oviparous with distinct pairing during reproduction; sexual maturity is reached around 31.8 cm. Captive reproduction has not been documented in typical home aquaria given the adult size and demanding husbandry, and is generally considered impractical outside specialised public-aquarium settings.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Painted Sweetlips product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/painted-sweetlips) provided scientific name Plectorhynchus pictus (an older synonym of Diagramma pictum), origin Indo-Pacific, listed max size 8 in (overview prose contradicts with 'up to 33 inches'), minimum tank 50 gal, care level Easy, temperament Peaceful, reef compatible Yes, diet Carnivore (krill/mysis/zooplankton/brine shrimp/crustaceans), temperature 72-78°F (22.2-25.6°C), pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025, and notes nocturnal bottom-feeder that disturbs substrate and threatens small fish/invertebrates.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed family Haemulidae, order Eupercaria/misc, max size 100 cm FL (common 55 cm), depth 1-50 m (recorded to ~170 m), preferred temperature 24.1-29.1°C (mean 28°C), maximum reported age 13 years, Indo-West Pacific distribution (Arabian Sea/Mozambique to Palau, Japan to New Caledonia), diet benthic invertebrates and fishes, oviparous with distinct pairing during spawning, marine/reef-associated.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Painted sweetlips)
additional.notes: Cross-checked size, range, and color phases: max length 100 cm (typical 55 cm, max weight 6.3 kg), Indo-Pacific from Red Sea/KwaZulu-Natal east to Fiji and north to Japan, depth to 80 m, juveniles black-and-white striped vs adults blue-grey with golden-yellow spots and orange-red mouth, diet benthic invertebrates and smaller fishes, distinct pairing during spawning, synonyms Perca picta/Plectorhinchus pictus/Spilotichthys pictus. Used in lieu of the playbook's first listed secondary (Reef2Reef wiki) which has historically returned HTTP 403 to WebFetch from this slice.

fish.breedingNotes: Wild spawning is oviparous with distinct pairing per FishBase; captive reproduction has not been documented for typical home aquaria given adult size and feeding requirements.
